content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue full structural delay of selection to age 14, or an incremental phase-down of only the earliest entry point, given the trade-off between depth of equity impact and feasibility of implementation?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can clarify the magnitude of variance reduction associated with full versus partial detracking in comparable systems (Poland, Finland) and can measure displacement effects at retained entry points. It cannot resolve how much political and legal risk a society should be willing to accept in exchange for a given equity gain, since that is a value judgment about acceptable disruption versus acceptable inequality, not an empirical fact.
content.tension: Full elimination of early tracking (age 10 and 12 entry points) offers the strongest theoretical alignment with cross-national inequality-reduction evidence, but carries the highest political, legal, and implementation risk; a partial reform targeting only the age-10 entry point reduces conflict scope but may simply displace rather than resolve segregation pressure.
content.title: Structural abolition versus incremental compromise in delaying selection
